original_hypothesis.title: Nonce Unchecked Overflow Allows Theoretical Replay After type(uint256).max Transactions
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Nonce._consumeNonce uses an unchecked block for nonce increment: `unchecked { uint256 newNonce = _nonce + 1; }`. At type(uint256).max, the nonce wraps to 0, enabling replay of every previously executed transaction in that nonce space. While reaching this limit requires 2^256 transactions (computationally impossible), the code defect violates the stated invariant of strict sequential nonce advancement.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The nonce overflow violates INV-STATE-003 (nonce must strictly increment) and INV-ECON-003 (nonce conservation). Though impractical, this represents a correctness defect. A simple `require(_nonce < type(uint256).max)` check would eliminate the theoretical risk at negligible gas cost.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { uint256 newNonce = _nonce + 1; } wraps at max uint256

preconditions: Nonce in a specific space reaches type(uint256).max (computationally impossible)
Attacker has stored valid signed transactions from nonce 0 onwards

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: uint256 newNonce = _nonce + 1 in unchecked context; at max wraps to 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After overflow, replay any historical signed transaction
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Theoretical replay of all historical transactions

economic_questions: Is 2^256 transactions achievable within any conceivable timeframe?
Could a future chain have different nonce semantics that reduce this number?
validation_requirements: Verify unchecked overflow behavior at type(uint256).max
Confirm no other overflow protection exists

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify unchecked addition wraps at max

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
